title: Two Sum
statement: Given a list of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two distinct elements whose values add up to target. Exactly one valid pair exists, and the indices may be returned in any order.
constraints: 2 <= len(nums) <= 10^4; values and target may be negative and as large as 10^9 in magnitude; exactly one solution exists. An O(n^2) approach is correct but may be too slow at the upper bound; O(n) is intended.

brute_force.approach: Try every pair of different positions. For each i, compare nums[i] with every later nums[j], and return [i, j] when their sum equals target. This is simple and correct because it checks all possible pairs without reusing an element.

optimal.approach: Scan the list once while keeping a mapping from each value already seen to its index. At position i, compute the value that would pair with nums[i] to make target. If that value has appeared earlier, return the earlier index and i. Otherwise, record nums[i] and continue.

key_insight: When looking at one number, the only possible partner value is target minus that number, so you can check whether that partner has already appeared instead of trying every pair.

traps.wrong_approach: Using the same element twice when nums[i] * 2 == target.
traps.why_wrong: The problem requires two distinct indices, even if one value alone could be doubled to reach the target.
traps.counterexample: nums = [3, 2, 4], target = 6; returning [0, 0] is invalid, while [1, 2] is correct.

traps.wrong_approach: Building a value-to-index mapping first, then allowing the current index to match itself.
traps.why_wrong: If the complement equals the current value, a single stored index may be the same element unless the code explicitly checks distinct indices.
traps.counterexample: nums = [3, 2, 4], target = 6; a lookup for 3 at index 0 can incorrectly pair index 0 with itself.

traps.wrong_approach: Using a set of values only and losing indices.
traps.why_wrong: The output must be indices, not values, so the solution needs to remember where each useful value occurred.
traps.counterexample: nums = [2, 7, 11, 15], target = 9; knowing that 2 and 7 exist is not enough to return [0, 1].

traps.wrong_approach: Sorting the array and using two pointers, then returning the sorted positions.
traps.why_wrong: Sorting changes positions, but the required answer is in the original array's indices unless original indices are preserved.
traps.counterexample: nums = [3, 2, 4], target = 6; sorted values are [2, 3, 4], whose pair positions are not the original answer [1, 2].

leak_terms: look up target minus current value
underlying_primitive: Fast membership and retrieval by key: given a value, quickly find whether it has been seen before and where.
